comments:
Summary:
The paper introduces "Graph Path PCA", a variant of sparse PCA in which the nonzero support of the principal component must form a path in a given directed acyclic graph. The authors study statistical sample-complexity under a layered (p,k,d)-graph model and the spiked covariance model, giving matching upper and lower bounds (up to constants). They propose two practical algorithms: a graph-truncated power method and a low-rank sample-and-project scheme; both rely on an efficient projection onto the path-constrained set that reduces to a longest-path computation on a DAG. Empirical results on synthetic data and two real datasets (finance and fMRI) illustrate improved interpretability and, in some regimes, improved recovery versus standard sparse PCA baselines.

Strengths:
- The problem formulation is natural and practically motivated: many applications require selecting structured supports (e.g., one-per-group or network-constrained selections), and encoding them as paths is a neat, flexible approach.
- The paper provides both an information-theoretic lower bound and an upper bound for a nontrivial graph model, demonstrating that graph side information can reduce sample complexity; matching (up to constants) lower/upper bounds are a solid theoretical contribution.
- The algorithms are simple, implementable, and leverage the DAG structure to make projection tractable; the reduction to longest path is elegant and yields linear-time projection in graph size.
- Experiments show interpretable components on real data and reasonable empirical performance vs. standard sparse PCA methods.

Weaknesses / Concerns:
- The theoretical analysis relies on a rather specific (p,k,d)-layered graph model with uniform layer sizes and degrees. While useful as a clean model, it is unclear how the bounds extend to more general graphs or how sensitive the results are to deviations from the idealized assumptions.
- Assumptions on the data are standard (spiked Gaussian covariance) but restrictive; practical data may violate them and the paper does not analyze robustness to model misspecification or to graph misspecification (incorrect/approximate graphs).
- Algorithmic guarantees are limited. The paper presents algorithms and describes their computational complexity, but provides no global convergence or approximation guarantees for the non-convex constrained optimization (apart from empirical evidence and discussion). The truncated power method can be sensitive to initialization; this is noted but not systematically addressed.
- Experimental comparisons are somewhat limited. Baselines include sparse PCA counterparts (truncated power and spannogram), but the paper omits other structured PCA approaches (e.g., methods using structured sparsity penalties, group penalties, or structured dictionary learning) that could be relevant. More details about hyperparameter selection, runtime, and sensitivity (e.g., choice of r in low-rank algorithm) would strengthen the empirical claims.
- The neuroscience experiment uses a graph constructed from Euclidean distances between ROIs; the authors correctly caution that this is not a causal connectivity graph, but more discussion/evidence on the validity of the induced supports and comparisons to domain knowledge or other network constructions would be valuable.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Analyze or at least discuss extensions of the theoretical results to more general graph families (e.g., variable layer sizes, irregular degree distributions, or DAGs that are not strictly layered).
- Provide more algorithmic analysis where possible (e.g., conditions under which the power method converges to a good solution, or bounds on how approximation in the low-rank step affects objective/subspace recovery).
- Expand experimental comparisons to other structured-sparsity PCA approaches and add sensitivity/runtimes to demonstrate scalability to larger p.
- Include experiments that study robustness to graph misspecification (e.g., perturb the graph edges and show performance degradation) since in many applications the graph is estimated or heuristic.
- Clean up minor presentation issues (some repeated paragraphs in the provided text) and make clear parameter choices in experiments.

Overall assessment:
This paper identifies an interesting and practical structured PCA problem, provides meaningful theoretical insight in a nontrivial model, and offers simple algorithms that exploit graph structure. Although the model assumptions and algorithmic guarantees are somewhat restrictive, the work opens a promising direction bridging structure/graph priors and dimensionality reduction. With additional empirical validation and broader analysis of graph families and algorithmic behavior, this line of work could be impactful for applications that require interpretable, network-constrained feature extraction.
